attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of intensely itchy patches on both legs. The patches started as small spots and have gradually enlarged. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He works as a builder and reports the itching is worse after showering. He recently moved into a share house that he describes as 'dirty' but does not have enough money to move locations. His father has psoriasis and his mother has severe hayfever.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple well-demarcated, coin-shaped, erythematous plaques on both lower legs. The lesions are dry and scaly with some excoriation marks. There is no yellow crusting or weeping.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of discoid (nummular) eczema: well-demarcated, coin-shaped (nummular) lesions that are intensely pruritic. The symmetrical distribution on the legs and chronic course are typical. The condition often worsens with frequent washing due to disruption of the skin barrier.

Discoid eczema typically affects adults (20 - 50 years of age) and presents with round or oval plaques that may be single or multiple. It is a chronic inflammatory skin disease. The lesions are usually symmetrically distributed on the limbs. The aetiology is unknown but may be related to dry skin, contact irritants, alcoholism or stress, and perhaps a variant of atopic dermatitis or related to psoriasis. Treatment is also with steroid cream.

Tinea corporis can present with round, scaly patches too, but typically has active borders with central clearing ('ring-worm' appearance). It is usually asymmetrical and would be confirmed with skin scrapings showing fungal elements.

Psoriasis presents with well-demarcated plaques but these are typically silvery-scaled and predominantly affect extensor surfaces, scalp, and natal cleft. The symmetrical distribution can be similar, but the scales in psoriasis are more silvery and thicker.

Scabies typically presents with intense nocturnal pruritus and characteristic burrows in the web spaces of fingers, wrists, and genitals. While the patient's living conditions might raise suspicion for scabies, the well-demarcated, coin-shaped lesions are not typical of scabies infestation.

Contact dermatitis would be expected to occur at sites of exposure to an irritant or allergen and would not typically present with such well-demarcated, coin-shaped lesions. The distribution would correspond to areas of contact with the triggering substance.
learningPoints: Discoid eczema typically presents with well-demarcated, coin-shaped lesions that are intensely pruritic.
The presence of symmetrical lesions on the limbs in an adult is characteristic of discoid eczema.
Secondary bacterial infection of discoid eczema should be considered when there is yellow crusting or weeping.
references: Bonamonte D, Filoni A, Gullo G, Vestita M. Nummular Contact Eczema: Presentation of a Pediatric Case.  The Open Dermatology Journal Case Report. 2019.  Available at: https://opendermatologyjournal.com/VOLUME/13/PAGE/23/FULLTEXT/

Böhner, Alexander et al. The neglected twin: Nummular eczema is a variant of atopic dermatitis with codominant TH2/TH17 immune response. Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology. 2023: 152 (2).  Available at: https://www.jacionline.org/article/S0091-6749(23)00514-6/fulltext
